Title: Discovery of key regulators of dark gland development and hypericin biosynthesis in St. John’s Wort (Hypericum perforatum)

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: dark glands of placental tissues (also dark glands in other organs)
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq: ENA PRJEB30287; metabolomics: UHPLC-ESI-HRMS/PDA accession NA
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly induced in dark gland-containing placental tissues during differentiation/maturation; represented by 4 DEGs in cluster 2
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly induced in G++ placental tissues, especially FB75
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Positively associated with hypericin biosynthesis and dark gland-associated naphthodianthrone accumulation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq differential expression between glanded and glandless placental tissues; metabolite correlation; prior literature expression association
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. POCP genes are strongly induced in dark gland-containing placental tissues. 2. They were previously discovered to be differentially expressed in dark gland-containing tissues and are recovered here among core DG-associated DEGs. 3. The study suggests they may participate in oxidative C-C bond formation between naphthodianthrone halves during hypericin biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate genes for engineering hypericin pathway in plants or microbes, but biochemical function remains unproven and should be combined with validated pathway steps.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function remains hypothetical; no direct biochemical proof in this paper. Authors suggest possible role in formation of C5-C5’ bond.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical compound; relevant to depression, cancer and Alzheimer’s disease research
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Developmental stage of placental tissue dark gland differentiation (FB45, FB75)

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Three DEGs in cluster 3 encode at least two ABCG transporters; highly induced in glanded placental tissues
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated during dark gland maturation, mainly FB75 in G++ placental tissues
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate transport functions associated with accumulation/sequestration of hypericin or related metabolites in dark glands
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. At least two ABCG transporter genes are strongly induced in dark gland-associated transcript clusters. 2. Their expression correlates with tissues accumulating hypericin and related metabolites. 3. They are proposed as candidate transporters for dark gland-associated metabolite movement/sequestration.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential accessory targets to improve storage/sequestration when engineering hypericin production.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Transport role is inferred; no direct transport assay provided.

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Encoded by DEGs in clusters 1, 2 and 3, highly induced in glanded placental tissues
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate transporter/trafficking accessory proteins for flavonoid and possibly naphthodianthrone transport
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq differential expression; pathway inference from literature
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GST genes are among highly induced DG-associated DEGs. 2. Their known roles in flavonoid transport suggest analogous involvement in naphthodianthrone sequestration. 3. Authors propose hypericin transport may involve GST-associated vesicles.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful accessory genes for hypericin metabolic engineering to support intracellular trafficking and sequestration.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No direct functional validation in this paper; included as accessory common genes rather than biosynthetic enzymes.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical compound; flavonoids are antioxidant metabolites

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly induced DEG in cluster 2 during DG maturation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Higher in G++ placental tissues, especially FB75
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate accessory oxidoreductase/coupling enzyme associated with late hypericin formation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq differential expression; functional inference from protein family
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A BBE-family DEG is highly induced in dark gland-containing placental tissue. 2. The study suggests it may provide an additional or alternative oxidative C-C coupling function in hypericin formation. 3. Secretory targeting suggests action in late bond closure steps.
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Secretory pathway/apoplast (predicted by N-terminal signal peptide)
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate late-step accessory gene for reconstructing hypericin biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Biochemical role remains hypothetical; authors compare it to cannabinoid oxidocyclases.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses malonyl-CoA units to form the octaketide precursor for hypericin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: first committed step; key control point suggested
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Three DEGs in cluster 2 align almost perfectly with different parts of HpPKS2; strongly induced during dark gland maturation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strongly upregulated in G++ placental tissues, mainly postdifferentiation (FB75)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes hypericin biosynthesis as the first dedicated biosynthetic step; expression tightly associated with glanded tissues accumulating hypericin
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq differential expression; sequence alignment to known HpPKS2; prior literature functional characterization of OKS
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Multiple DG-associated DEGs correspond to HpPKS2. 2. HpPKS2 expression is highly induced specifically in glanded placental tissues during stages accumulating hypericin. 3. The gene encodes the octaketide synthase responsible for the first committed step in hypericin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential core enzyme for hypericin pathway engineering and a prime target for boosting or abolishing hypericin production.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Previously characterized in literature; this study provides tissue/developmental association with dark glands.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pharmaceutical compound with relevance to depression, cancer and Alzheimer’s disease research

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Proposed to catalyze correct cyclization of the octaketide generated by HpPKS2
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly induced in cluster 2 in glanded placental tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate missing enzymatic step enabling correct precursor cyclization for hypericin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq differential expression; functional inference from annotation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A highly induced putative polyketide cyclase was identified among dark gland-associated DEGs. 2. The study proposes it may provide a missing cyclization function in hypericin precursor formation. 3. Its timing of expression matches active hypericin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important candidate to include when reconstructing the pathway beyond HpPKS2 alone.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No biochemical validation in this paper.

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: putative thioesterase (annotated as dihydrofolate reductase homolog)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Proposed to release octaketide and tetraketide precursors from coenzyme A conjugates
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Two DEGs in clusters 2 and 3 induced in glanded placental tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate support enzymes for precursor liberation feeding hypericin and flavonoid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq differential expression; annotation-based inference
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Two dark gland-associated DEGs were annotated as dihydrofolate reductases but belong to a serine hydrolase superfamily. 2. Authors propose they may act as thioesterases releasing hypericin/flavonoid precursors from CoA conjugates. 3. Their induction coincides with dark gland-associated metabolic activation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential helper enzymes for pathway reconstitution, but uncertain annotation requires validation.

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Up-regulation starts earlier than most biosynthesis-associated genes, consistent with activation of dark gland developmental program that enables downstream hypericin biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Putative key regulator/master developmental switch for dark gland differentiation; decisive because expression is strictly synchronized with gland primordium formation
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely MADS-box transcriptional complexes; specific partners not validated
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly upregulated already at FB45; at FB75 average transcript levels at least 100-fold higher in G++ than G- lines
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strictly synchronized with beginning of dark gland differentiation in placental tissue
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely promotes dark gland formation, thereby enabling hypericin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq differential expression across developmental stages and glanded/glandless genotypes
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AGL6 is one of only two transcription factors whose expression is strictly synchronized with dark gland differentiation. 2. Its transcription rises dramatically at FB45 and reaches over 100-fold higher levels in glanded placental tissues by FB75. 3. The study proposes AGL6 as a key regulator of dark gland development, which precedes hypericin biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Prime target to hyperactivate or shut down dark gland formation and thereby modulate hypericin content in breeding or engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function is inferred from expression timing and homology; no transgenic validation yet. Authors suggest possible neofunctionalization in Hypericum.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulating gland development may alter floral organ development due to known AGL6 roles in organ identity

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: R2R3-MYB transcription factor; MYB38/RAX2 homolog, subgroup 14-like
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: dark gland patterning/development preceding hypericin biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Likely activates developmental program for dark gland emergence; downstream biosynthetic genes are induced later
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Putative key regulator of dark gland formation based on strict synchronization with DG differentiation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strongly upregulated in cluster 1 already at FB45 in glanded placental tissues
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strictly synchronized with onset of dark gland differentiation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely promotes placental dark gland formation and indirectly hypericin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative RNA-seq differential expression and homology-based annotation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. A MYB38/RAX2-like R2R3-MYB is one of two transcription factors tightly linked to the onset of dark gland differentiation. 2. Its early induction in glanded placentas suggests a regulatory role before activation of most biosynthetic genes. 3. The study hypothesizes that it regulates the lateral patterning process from which dark glands emerge.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate to engineer gland number and hypericin accumulation by controlling gland developmental patterning.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Homology indicates relation to cell fate/organ formation regulators rather than direct secondary metabolism regulators.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May affect organ patterning or development beyond glands
